metadata.title: Prefill: Control Claude's Output Format
sources: Anthropic Prompt Engineering Interactive Tutorial — Chapter 5: Formatting Output and Speaking for Claude

beats.narration_text: Prefilling Claude's response to lock its output format is a trick I want to actually master, not just admire. Help me: (1) explain how starting the assistant turn with an opening brace or a tag forces structured output and suppresses preamble; (2) write a prompt-plus-prefill pair that guarantees valid JSON with three named keys; (3) show me one case where a prefill backfires — the model breaks format anyway or the prefill contradicts the instruction — and how I'd repair it.

props.command: Prefilling Claude's response to lock its output format is a trick I want to actually master, not just admire. Help me: (1) explain how starting the assistant turn with an opening brace or a tag forces structured output and suppresses preamble; (2) write a prompt-plus-prefill pair that guarantees valid JSON with three named keys; (3) show me one case where a prefill backfires — the model breaks format anyway or the prefill contradicts the instruction — and how I'd repair it.
output: Prefill the assistant turn with partial output to lock format
Opening '{' forces JSON — Claude continues the structure
Opening '<haiku>' forces the XML tag wrapper every time
stop_sequences cuts output at a closing tag — saves tokens

beats.narration_text: Let's recap with Claude. The messages array has three roles: system, user, and assistant. Most developers only write to user. Prefilling means you add one more entry — role assistant, content your partial output — before sending the request. Claude picks up from wherever you stopped. It's not a prompt hack. It's a documented feature of the Messages API.

props.artifactHeading: Add a partial assistant turn — Claude continues from there.
artifactLines: messages: [{role: 'user', content: prompt}, {role: 'assistant', content: '<haiku>'}]
Claude sees '<haiku>' already written and must continue it
Output starts mid-tag — no preamble, no 'Sure, here's a haiku:'
Documented feature of the Anthropic Messages API
Works in Python SDK, TypeScript SDK, and raw API calls

beats.narration_text: Format forcing in practice. Prefill with open curly brace — you get JSON, every time. Prefill with 'one period' — you get a numbered list. Prefill with the opening XML tag — you get that tag wrapping the output, no extra text. The preamble problem that trips up beginners — 'Sure! Here's your haiku' before the actual haiku — simply disappears.

artifactLines: Prefill '{' → JSON object, keys and structure guaranteed
Prefill '1.' → numbered list, no preamble
Prefill '<haiku>' → XML-wrapped output only
Eliminates 'Sure! Here's your answer:' opener entirely
No regex cleanup needed — format is locked at generation time

beats.narration_text: Important caveat: this behavior changed in Claude 4 models. The Anthropic tutorial flags it explicitly. Newer Claude models handle prefill differently than older ones — the mechanism still works but you should test against your target model version. If you're on Claude 4.6 or later, verify behavior before deploying a pipeline that depends on strict prefill output.

props.artifactHeading: Prefill behavior shifted — test against your target version.
artifactLines: Anthropic tutorial flags this change explicitly in the notebook
Older Claude 3 models: prefill strictly deterministic
Claude 4+ models: prefill still works but behavior differs
Always test prefill output on your target model before shipping
Use structured output or tool_use for strict JSON in production

beats.narration_text: The complementary technique: stop sequences. You pass a closing tag — say, closing haiku tag — to the stop_sequences parameter. Claude generates until it hits that string and then stops. No concluding remarks. No 'I hope you enjoyed this poem.' You save tokens and time-to-last-token, and your parser gets a clean end marker. Prefill opens the format; stop sequences close it.

props.artifactHeading: Prefill opens. stop_sequences closes. Parser gets clean output.
artifactLines: stop_sequences=['</haiku>'] — Claude halts at that marker
Eliminates 'I hope you enjoyed this poem!' closing remarks
Saves tokens: billing stops when generation stops
Reduces time-to-last-token in streaming pipelines
Combine with prefill: locked open + locked close = exact output

beats.narration_text: Your turn. Ask Claude: 'Write a JSON object with keys title, summary, and tags for this article: [paste any article text]. Prefill with open brace.' The point is to watch what happens when you add that prefill versus when you don't. Without it, you get a sentence first. With it, Claude opens with the brace and the JSON starts immediately. That delta — no preamble, machine-parseable output — is worth understanding before you build any pipeline that processes Claude responses programmatically. Liam, in for Bear.

props.command: Write a JSON object with keys title, summary, and tags for this article: [paste text]. Prefill with {
output: Output begins immediately with the JSON object — no preamble
Compare with and without prefill to see the delta clearly
Add stop_sequences to cut trailing remarks after closing brace
